Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to catch up, but with my extracurriculars and everything else going on, it rarely worked out as planned. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often spend time doing practice problems or working through things physically rather than just reading. I probably averaged about 9 hours a week, but it was scattered across random pockets of time whenever I could squeeze it in.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due. Some weeks I barely touched anything, and then right before a deadline I'd be cramming hard. I tried to be consistent, but life kept getting in the way—club meetings, part-time work stuff, social commitments. The regularity just wasn't there, even though I wanted it to be.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed. I wasn't one of those people who checked it daily—more like when I remembered or when panic set in about an assignment.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. There were brief periods where I'd check it more regularly, usually early in the semester when motivation was higher. But it never stuck as part of my daily or even weekly routine—it always felt like something I had to force myself to do.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty useful when I watched it. I'd let them play while I worked through problems hands-on, which helped things click better for me. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful too when other students explained concepts in different ways. Anything that let me actively do something rather than just passively read felt more valuable.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They just didn't match how I learn—I need to be doing something, not just reading about it. I'd skim them at best or download them with good intentions and never open them again. The forums I also didn't engage with much because typing out responses felt tedious.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or practice something concrete. If I could work through a problem and actually feel like I understood it better afterward, that was valuable. Stuff that felt theoretical or disconnected from actual application—I'd lose interest fast. I needed that hands-on connection.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like they were negotiable, which meant they usually got cut when time was tight. Required stuff I'd force myself through even if I wasn't into it, but optional things were the first to go. It's not that I didn't see their value—I just couldn't justify the time when I was already struggling to complete the mandatory work.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of materials and worked through them on paper or with physical objects when I could. I'd print out problem sets and work through them by hand, or I'd find YouTube videos that explained things in more practical ways. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems at my desk with printed notes, or I'd be discussing concepts with classmates in person. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also did a lot of mental processing while doing other activities—like figuring out problems while at the gym or during my commute.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd download lecture slides or problem sets once, maybe watch a video once, and then work with those materials offline for hours. The LMS would just show that one access, but I might have spent a whole afternoon with that content printed out on my desk.
Q12: Around midterms things got really tough. I had a lot of competing deadlines from other courses and my extracurricular commitments ramped up. Even though my anxiety stayed pretty low, the stress of juggling everything made it hard to engage deeply with any single course. I felt spread thin and couldn't give this course the attention it needed.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or overwhelmed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Competing responsibilities from my activities and other courses meant I'd often just do the bare minimum to get by. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections rather than pushing through—I'd tell myself I'd come back to it later.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing in the background while my mind was elsewhere, or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I was just too mentally exhausted to engage, but I'd go through the motions anyway hoping something would stick. It rarely did in those moments.
Q15: I postponed a lot of the heavier assignments, especially when they seemed overwhelming or when I wasn't sure where to start. Even knowing they were important, the uncertainty about how to approach them made me push them off. I'd convince myself I'd be more prepared later, but later usually meant scrambling at the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have these bursts of engagement right before something was due, then go quiet again. It wasn't healthy or effective, but it was how I operated. The deadline pressure was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my other obligations and force me to focus.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did motivate me to adjust my approach, at least temporarily. Positive feedback made me feel like I was on the right track, and critical feedback helped me see where I needed to focus. But honestly, feedback often came too late for me to really incorporate it into the next thing—I was already behind by then.
Q18: The structure felt a bit rigid for my learning style. I do better with more flexibility and hands-on components, but the course was pretty lecture and reading heavy. Having 13 online courses to manage also made it hard to give proper attention to any single one. I needed more practical application built into the structure.
Q19: The video content was helpful, but I wished there were more interactive elements or lab-type activities. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic. What would have helped is more scaffolding for the bigger assignments—breaking them into smaller checkpoints rather than one big deadline. That might have kept me more consistently engaged.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying to figure things out but struggling to produce quality work. They might be exploring, clicking around, watching videos multiple times because they're confused—but the actual output doesn't materialize because they haven't truly grasped the material. Activity doesn't equal understanding.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat, though my performance wasn't great this time. But I know the LMS doesn't capture everything—someone could be studying effectively offline and only popping into the system to submit work. My low login frequency doesn't mean I wasn't trying; it means I was learning in different ways.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're genuinely learning or just going through motions. They also miss all the offline work, the conversations with peers, the thinking that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when you're actively wrestling with concepts, when you're making connections. For me, it's most real when I'm doing something hands-on—when learning feels active rather than passive. Just logging in doesn't mean I'm engaged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was trying to balance everything, or how I was genuinely working through problems on paper away from the computer. It wouldn't capture my discussions with classmates or the time I spent thinking through concepts during other activities. My engagement was real, just not always visible in the digital traces.
